title: Explaining changes in US residential segregation through patterns of population change

data_scope.spatial_units: census blocks / block groups / tracts (harmonized via NHGIS crosswalks)

core_conclusion: US residential segregation declined from 1990 to 2020, but asymmetrically: almost all of the decrease was produced by suburbanization and central-city growth of Black, Hispanic and Asian populations, while shifts in the white population mostly increased segregation. Cities undergo simultaneous desegregation and resegregation ('racialized reshuffling') that operates within suburbs, so integrating minorities without limiting white resegregation will not produce lasting reductions.
why_it_matters: Attributes change in segregation to specific racial groups and specific places (not just measuring levels); reframes policy toward limiting majority-group resegregation; reveals intra-suburban sorting; ships a reusable, open decomposition method with full OSF replication package.
why_nature: Couples population dynamics to urban spatial structure; generalizable decomposition method across 224 metros over 30 years; counter-intuitive, policy-relevant finding; fully reproducible from public census data.
added_value_to_my_research: Mobility-based exposure decomposition -> equity diagnostic for realized health-service access
Geographically weighted (MGWR) entropy decomposition -> local resegregation hotspots and reusable for amenity access
Explainable ML (SHAP) on conservation-respecting decomposition labels -> drivers of reshuffling tied to housing-price/accessibility features
Linking segregation-change components to 2SFCA primary-care/mental-health access in the same MSAs
Additive decomposition as a conservation prior in a physics-informed graph operator for flux-on-network problems
